content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>

<html:p>We've learned how important inlining is to optimisation, but also that it might sometimes cause code bloat. Inlining doesn't have to be all-or-nothing!</html:p>

<html:p>Let's look at a simple function that has a fast path and slow path; and then see how the compiler handles it<html:sup id="fnref:note"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:note">1</html:a></html:sup>.</html:p>

<html:p>In this example we have some <html:code>process</html:code> function that has a really trivial fast case for numbers in the range 0-100. For other numbers it does something more expensive. Then <html:code>compute</html:code> calls <html:code>process</html:code> twice (making it less appealing to inline all of <html:code>process</html:code>).</html:p>

<html:p>Looking at the assembly output, we see what's happened: The compiler has split <html:code>process</html:code> into two functions, a <html:code>process (part.0)</html:code> that does the expensive part only. It then rewrites <html:code>process</html:code> into the quick check for 100, returning double the value if less than 100. If not, it jumps to the <html:code>(part.0)</html:code> function:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nf">process</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">):</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">99</html:span><html:span class="w">                           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; less than or equal to 99?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jbe</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L7</html:span><html:span class="w">                               </html:span><html:span class="c1">; skip to fast path if so</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">process</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">(.</html:span><html:span class="no">part.0</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span><html:span class="w">   </html:span><html:span class="c1">; else jump to the expensive path</html:span>
<html:span class="nl">.L7:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">lea</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">                    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; return `value * 2`</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ret</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>This first step - extracting the cold path into a separate function - is called <html:strong>function outlining</html:strong>. The original <html:code>process</html:code> becomes a thin wrapper handling the hot path, delegating to the outlined <html:code>process (.part.0)</html:code> when needed. This split sets up the real trick: <html:strong>partial inlining</html:strong>. When the compiler later inlines <html:code>process</html:code> into <html:code>compute</html:code>, it inlines just the wrapper whilst keeping calls to the outlined cold path. External callers can still call <html:code>process</html:code> and have it work correctly for all values.</html:p>

<html:p>Let's see this optimisation in action in the <html:code>compute</html:code> function:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nf">compute</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">):</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">99</html:span><html:span class="w">                   </html:span><html:span class="c1">; is a <= 99?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jbe</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L13</html:span><html:span class="w">                      </html:span><html:span class="c1">; if so, go to the inlined fast path for a</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">call</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">process</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">(.</html:span><html:span class="no">part.0</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="c1">; else, call expensive case</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; save the result of process(a)</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">esi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">99</html:span><html:span class="w">                   </html:span><html:span class="c1">; is b <= 99?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jbe</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L14</html:span><html:span class="w">                      </html:span><html:span class="c1">; if so go to the inlined fast path for b</html:span>
<html:span class="nl">.L11:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">esi</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; otherwise, call expensive case for b</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">call</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">process</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">(.</html:span><html:span class="no">part.0</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; add the two slow cases together</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ret</html:span><html:span class="w">                           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; return</html:span>

<html:span class="nl">.L13:</html:span><html:span class="w">                           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; case where a is fast case</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">lea</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">            </html:span><html:span class="c1">; process(a) is just a + a</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">esi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">99</html:span><html:span class="w">                   </html:span><html:span class="c1">; is b > 99?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ja</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L11</html:span><html:span class="w">                       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; jump to b slow case if so</html:span>
<html:span class="w">                                </html:span><html:span class="c1">; (falls through to...)</html:span>
<html:span class="nl">.L14:</html:span><html:span class="w">                           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; b fast case</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">lea</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">            </html:span><html:span class="c1">; double b</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="w">                  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; return 2*a + 2*b</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ret</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>Looking at <html:code>compute</html:code>, we can see the benefits of this approach clearly: The simple range check and arithmetic (<html:code>cmp</html:code>, <html:code>lea</html:code>) are inlined directly, avoiding the function call overhead for the fast path. When a value is 100 or greater, it calls the outlined <html:code>process (.part.0)</html:code> function for the more expensive computation.</html:p>

<html:p>This is the best of both worlds: we get the performance benefit of inlining the lightweight check and simple arithmetic, whilst avoiding code bloat from duplicating the expensive computation<html:sup id="fnref:again"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:again">2</html:a></html:sup>. The original <html:code>process</html:code> function remains intact and callable, so external callers still work correctly.</html:p>

<html:p>Partial inlining lets the compiler make nuanced trade-offs about what to inline and what to keep shared. The compiler can outline portions of a function based on its heuristics about code size and performance<html:sup id="fnref:tradeoff"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:tradeoff">3</html:a></html:sup>, giving you benefits of inlining without necessarily paying the full code size cost. In this example, the simple check is duplicated whilst the complex computation stays shared.</html:p>

<html:p>As with many optimisations, the compiler's heuristics<html:sup id="fnref:clang"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:clang">4</html:a></html:sup> usually make reasonable choices about when to apply partial inlining, but it's worth checking your hot code paths to see if the compiler has made the decisions you expect. Taking a quick peek in <html:a href="https://godbolt.org">Compiler Explorer</html:a> is a good way to develop your intuition.</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>See <html:a href="https://youtu.be/STZb5K5sPDs">the video</html:a> that accompanies this post.</html:em></html:p>

<html:hr />

<html:p><html:em>This post is day 18 of <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025</html:a>,
a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>← <html:a href="/202512/17-inlining-the-ultimate-optimisation">Inlining - the ultimate optimisation</html:a> | <html:a href="/202512/19-tail-call-optimisation">Chasing your tail</html:a> →</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>Support Compiler Explorer on <html:a href="https://patreon.com/c/mattgodbolt">Patreon</html:a>
or <html:a href="https://github.com/sponsors/compiler-explorer">GitHub</html:a>,
or by buying CE products in the <html:a href="https://shop.compiler-explorer.com">Compiler Explorer Shop</html:a></html:em>.</html:p>

<html:div class="footnote">
<html:hr />
<html:ol>
<html:li id="fn:note">
<html:p>I have had to cheat a little here to get the output I want: I've actually disabled GCC's main inlining pass, otherwise it chooses to inline the whole of <html:code>process</html:code>. With a larger, more complex "slow path" that would be unnecessary, but in order to demonstrate the effect of partial inlining without generating tons of code, I'm using this slight cheat. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:note" title="Jump back to footnote 1 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:again">
<html:p>Again, in this contrived example it probably <html:em>would</html:em> be OK to inline <html:code>process</html:code>, and the compiler really wants to, but for didactic purposes I've prevented that here. You can hopefully get the gist of this. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:again" title="Jump back to footnote 2 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:tradeoff">
<html:p>Of course, nothing is free - duplicating code still takes up instruction cache space. The compiler's heuristics have to weigh the benefits against the costs, and different compilers make different choices. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:tradeoff" title="Jump back to footnote 3 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:clang">
<html:p>Note that this varies substantially from compiler to compiler: I couldn't trick clang into making similar partial inlining decisions to gcc using flags, so I couldn't compare like with like. In my experience gcc and clang make quite different choices about inlining. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:clang" title="Jump back to footnote 4 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
</html:ol>
</html:div>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. We've learned how important inlining is to optimisation, but also that it might sometimes cause code bloat. Inlining doesn't have to be all-or-nothing! Let's look at a simple function that has a fast path and slow path; and then see how the compiler handles it 1 . In this example we have some process function that has a really trivial fast case for numbers in the range 0-100. For other numbers it does something more expensive. Then compute calls process twice (making it less appealing to inline all of process ). Looking at the assembly output, we see what's happened: The compiler has split process into two functions, a process (part.0) that does the expensive part only. It then rewrites process into the quick check for 100, returning double the value if less than 100. If not, it jumps to the (part.0) function: process ( unsigned int ): cmp edi , 99 ; less than or equal to 99? jbe .L7 ; skip to fast path if so jmp process ( unsigned int ) (. part.0 ) ; else jump to the expensive path .L7: lea eax , [ rdi + rdi ] ; return `value * 2` ret This first step - extracting the cold path into a separate function - is called function outlining . The original process becomes a thin wrapper handling the hot path, delegating to the outlined process (.part.0) when needed. This split sets up the real trick: partial inlining . When the compiler later inlines process into compute , it inlines just the wrapper whilst keeping calls to the outlined cold path. External callers can still call process and have it work correctly for all values. Let's see this optimisation in action in the compute function: compute ( unsigned int , unsigned int ): cmp edi , 99 ; is a <= 99? jbe .L13 ; if so, go to the inlined fast path for a call process ( unsigned int ) (. part.0 ) ; else, call expensive case mov r8d , eax ; save the result of process(a) cmp esi , 99 ; is b <= 99? jbe .L14 ; if so go to the inlined fast path for b .L11: mov edi , esi ; otherwise, call expensive case for b call process ( unsigned int ) (. part.0 ) add eax , r8d ; add the two slow cases together ret ; return .L13: ; case where a is fast case lea r8d , [ rdi + rdi ] ; process(a) is just a + a cmp esi , 99 ; is b > 99? ja .L11 ; jump to b slow case if so ; (falls through to...) .L14: ; b fast case lea eax , [ rsi + rsi ] ; double b add eax , r8d ; return 2*a + 2*b ret Looking at compute , we can see the benefits of this approach clearly: The simple range check and arithmetic ( cmp , lea ) are inlined directly, avoiding the function call overhead for the fast path. When a value is 100 or greater, it calls the outlined process (.part.0) function for the more expensive computation. This is the best of both worlds: we get the performance benefit of inlining the lightweight check and simple arithmetic, whilst avoiding code bloat from duplicating the expensive computation 2 . The original process function remains intact and callable, so external callers still work correctly. Partial inlining lets the compiler make nuanced trade-offs about what to inline and what to keep shared. The compiler can outline portions of a function based on its heuristics about code size and performance 3 , giving you benefits of inlining without necessarily paying the full code size cost. In this example, the simple check is duplicated whilst the complex computation stays shared. As with many optimisations, the compiler's heuristics 4 usually make reasonable choices about when to apply partial inlining, but it's worth checking your hot code paths to see if the compiler has made the decisions you expect. Taking a quick peek in Compiler Explorer is a good way to develop your intuition. See the video that accompanies this post. This post is day 18 of Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025 , a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code. ← Inlining - the ultimate optimisation | Chasing your tail → This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans. Support Compiler Explorer on Patreon or GitHub , or by buying CE products in the Compiler Explorer Shop . I have had to cheat a little here to get the output I want: I've actually disabled GCC's main inlining pass, otherwise it chooses to inline the whole of process . With a larger, more complex "slow path" that would be unnecessary, but in order to demonstrate the effect of partial inlining without generating tons of code, I'm using this slight cheat. ↩ Again, in this contrived example it probably would be OK to inline process , and the compiler really wants to, but for didactic purposes I've prevented that here. You can hopefully get the gist of this. ↩ Of course, nothing is free - duplicating code still takes up instruction cache space. The compiler's heuristics have to weigh the benefits against the costs, and different compilers make different choices. ↩ Note that this varies substantially from compiler to compiler: I couldn't trick clang into making similar partial inlining decisions to gcc using flags, so I couldn't compare like with like. In my experience gcc and clang make quite different choices about inlining. ↩
summary: Inlining doesn't have to be all-or-nothing
title: Partial inlining
